sections.title: Safe Sleep Fundamentals (SAFE T)
sections.summary: The ABCs of safe sleep prevent SIDS and sleep-related infant death.
tips: A — Alone: baby sleeps alone in their own sleep space (no bed-sharing with adults)
B — Back: always place baby on their back to sleep — every single time
C — Crib: firm, flat sleep surface in a safety-approved crib, bassinet, or play yard
No loose items in the sleep space: no blankets, pillows, bumpers, stuffed animals, or positioners
Swaddle or sleep sack replaces blankets safely — stop swaddling once baby shows signs of rolling
Room-sharing (baby in same room, not same bed) is recommended for at least the first 6 months and ideally 1 year
Use a firm, flat mattress with a fitted sheet — nothing else
warnings: Never place baby to sleep on a sofa, armchair, bouncy seat, or swing unsupervised
Car seats are for travel only — transfer baby to a flat surface when you get home
Sleeping in a bouncer or inclined surface is not safe for unattended sleep

sections.title: Setting Up the Sleep Space
sections.summary: The right environment signals sleep and helps baby stay asleep longer.
tips: Darkness: blackout curtains for daytime naps — light is the main signal that keeps babies awake
White noise: 65–70 dB (about the level of a shower) placed at least 2 metres from baby
Temperature: 18–20°C (64–68°F) — slightly cool is better than warm for safe sleep
Dress baby appropriately: 1 more layer than you would wear, or use a sleep sack rated for the room temp
White noise types that work well: fan, rain, pink noise, or a dedicated machine (avoid music or nature sounds with gaps)
If using a swaddle: baby's hips should be able to move freely — tight swaddling of hips causes hip dysplasia
warnings: Overheating is a SIDS risk factor — check that baby's chest is warm but not sweaty
Avoid sleeping baby near a heat vent or in direct sunlight

sections.title: Fixing Day/Night Confusion
sections.summary: Newborns are often 'flipped' for the first 2–4 weeks — you can gradually correct this.
tips: During the day: let in bright natural light, make normal household noise, engage with baby when awake
At night: keep lights very dim (use a red nightlight — red light has the least impact on melatonin)
Night feeds: minimise interaction — no talking, eye contact, or stimulation
Short daytime naps (cap at 2 hours) help consolidate night sleep — this matters more after 6 weeks
Take baby outside in natural daylight each day — morning light especially helps set their circadian rhythm
Consistent bedtime routine (even a simple 10-minute version) can start as early as 6–8 weeks

sections.title: Age-Appropriate Wake Windows
sections.summary: Putting an overtired baby down is harder — timing the window is everything.
tips: 0–4 weeks: 45–60 minutes of awake time maximum (this includes the feed!)
4–8 weeks: 60–90 minutes between sleeps
2–3 months: 75–90 minutes, lengthening toward end of the period
3 months: 90 minutes–2 hours
Watch for tired cues: yawning, glazed eyes, slowing movements, turning away from stimulation, fussing
Start your wind-down routine when you see the FIRST yawn — don't wait for crying
Newborns can only handle about 1 hour of total stimulation before needing sleep
warnings: Overtired babies fight sleep hardest — they get a cortisol surge that makes it harder to settle
Undertired babies won't sleep well either — there's a window, and it's narrow early on

sections.title: Building a Sleep Routine
sections.summary: A predictable pre-sleep routine becomes a powerful sleep cue within 1–2 weeks.
tips: Keep it short (10–15 minutes) and consistent — same order every time
Example routine: dim lights → nappy change → swaddle/sleep sack → feed (if needed) → gentle rocking → white noise → put down
Do the routine in the sleep space so the environment itself becomes the cue
Same routine for naps and bedtime helps reinforce the association
Bedtime for newborns is often 9–10pm — this shifts earlier (6:30–8pm) around 3–4 months as biology changes
A full feed before sleep helps baby stay asleep longer

sections.title: Swaddling Technique
sections.summary: A proper swaddle suppresses the Moro (startle) reflex and is one of the most effective newborn sleep tools.
tips: Place a square muslin at a diamond angle, fold down the top corner, lay baby with shoulders at the fold
One arm down straight (or slightly bent), wrap that side across and tuck firmly behind their back
Fold the bottom up over the feet (hips should be able to flex and spread)
Wrap the second side across and tuck in
Snug around the chest but you should be able to fit 2 fingers at the chest
Velcro swaddles (Love to Dream, Halo, Ergobaby) are easier to use correctly and stay put
Test the swaddle: lift baby — if it unravels, it won't survive sleep
warnings: Stop swaddling as soon as baby shows signs of rolling — usually around 8–16 weeks
Never swaddle with arms up in a traditional swaddle — this increases risk of hip dysplasia
Arms-up swaddles (Love to Dream) are designed for that position safely

sections.title: Contact Naps & Independent Sleep
sections.summary: Contact naps are normal and sometimes the only option in the early weeks — they don't 'create bad habits' at this age.
tips: 0–3 months: contact naps (napping on you) are developmentally normal and fine
Biologically, newborns expect contact — this is not a 'bad habit' at this stage
If you want to transition from contact to cot: try placing baby down after a deeper sleep cycle (15–20 min into nap)
'Dream feed' technique: keep them drowsy but partially awake as you lay them down — they learn to self-settle gradually
A warm surface (pre-warmed with a heat pack, removed before laying baby down) can help with the transfer
If contact naps are working and everyone is resting — lean into them in the early weeks

sections.title: Managing Overnight Wakings
sections.summary: Night feeds are biologically necessary for months — the goal is to make them as efficient as possible.
tips: Newborns (0–3 months) need to feed every 2–4 hours overnight — this is normal and expected
Keep night feeds boring: dim light, minimal talking, no stimulating eye contact
Change nappy before the feed (not after) — this helps baby stay drowsy after feeding
Use a red nightlight rather than a bright white light
Have everything ready before you pick baby up: bottle warmed, nipple shield ready, burp cloth in place
Take turns with your partner — one person per night (or per half of the night) allows longer sleep blocks
Sleep when baby sleeps during the day — this advice is clichéd but true in the first 6 weeks

sections.title: Short Naps (The 45-Minute Intruder)
sections.summary: Naps ending at exactly 30–45 minutes are a normal developmental pattern, not a problem you caused.
tips: Babies complete one sleep cycle in about 45 minutes — many wake fully between cycles
They haven't yet learned to link cycles independently — this usually develops at 4–5 months
Try: being present at the 35-minute mark to gently shush/pat through the transition before full waking
Motion naps (pram, baby carrier) often extend naturally because motion helps link cycles
Don't stress about short naps in the first 3 months — quantity over quality
Some babies are naturally short nappers throughout infancy and do fine with more frequent naps

sections.title: Sleep Regressions
sections.summary: Sleep regressions coincide with developmental leaps and are temporary — usually 2–4 weeks.
tips: Common regression points: 4 weeks, 8 weeks, 4 months (the biggest one), 8 months, 12 months
The 4-month regression is the most significant — baby's sleep architecture permanently changes to adult-like cycles
During a regression: more feeds, shorter naps, more night waking is normal
Survival strategies: lean on contact naps, add an extra feed, lower expectations temporarily
Regressions end — maintaining the routine through them helps baby return to baseline faster
Introducing new sleep associations during a regression can make them permanent (e.g. feeding to sleep every waking)

sections.title: Baby Will Only Sleep on You
sections.summary: This is incredibly common and doesn't mean you've done something wrong.
tips: Your smell, heartbeat, and warmth are literally what baby has known for 9 months — of course they prefer you
Try the warm sheet trick: lay a worn (unwashed) t-shirt under baby so they can smell you
Feed on the mattress next to the crib and transfer sideways rather than lowering from above
Baby carriers and wraps allow you to have your hands free during contact naps
If you need to put baby down: a firm swaddle + white noise + slight incline (safe wedge under mattress) can help
This phase does pass — it is hardest around weeks 2–6 and gradually improves
whenToCall: Baby can't be put down at all and this is causing safety concerns (parent falling asleep with baby on unsafe surface)
Baby seems in pain when lying flat — could indicate reflux

sections.title: Reflux & Sleep
sections.summary: Reflux causes pain when lying flat and disrupts sleep significantly.
tips: Keep baby upright for 20–30 minutes after every feed before laying down
Smaller, more frequent feeds reduce the volume in the stomach at one time
Burp thoroughly mid-feed and after
A slight incline on the sleep surface (safe wedge under the mattress — not under the baby) can help
Gripe water and gas drops can help with gas-related discomfort, not reflux specifically
warnings: Never prop the mattress with a folded blanket or towel under the baby — this is a suffocation risk
Silent reflux (no visible spit-up but signs of pain: arching back, pulling off breast, crying after feeds) is common and treatable
whenToCall: Baby arching back and crying inconsolably after feeds consistently
Weight not gaining appropriately due to feeding discomfort
Projectile vomiting more than occasionally after feeds

sections.title: The Eat-Play-Sleep Framework
sections.summary: A simple daytime structure that builds predictability and prevents feeding from becoming the only way baby falls asleep.
tips: The cycle: baby wakes → feed → awake play time → sleep. Repeat.
Feeding at wake (not at the end of the awake period) means the feed and falling asleep are separated — baby learns to fall asleep without a feed in their mouth
This makes overnight wakings much easier — baby doesn't need to feed every time they surface between sleep cycles
The 'play' phase for a newborn is just being awake: a nappy change, a few minutes of face-to-face time, tummy time on your chest — it doesn't need to be stimulating
EPS works best from about 3–4 weeks; in the first 2 weeks don't try to impose any schedule
The length of each cycle stretches naturally as wake windows grow: ~2 hours at newborn → ~3.5 hours at 3 months
Night feeds: keep the EPS principle loose at night — feed, minimal stimulation, back to sleep. Don't force play at 3am.
EPS is a framework, not a rigid schedule — a hungry baby should always be fed
warnings: Applying EPS too strictly in the first 2–3 weeks can interfere with milk supply and weight gain — newborns need to feed on demand
If baby falls asleep mid-feed regularly, the wake window may be too long or baby may be overtired — shorten it

sections.title: Naps & Wake Windows by Age
sections.summary: A reference table for how much awake time and how many naps to expect at each stage.
tips: Newborn (0–4 weeks): wake window 45–60 min, 4–5+ naps per day, total sleep ~16–18 hrs
1 month: wake window 45–75 min, 4–5 naps per day, total sleep ~15–17 hrs
2 months: wake window 45–90 min, 4–5 naps per day, total sleep ~14–16 hrs
3 months: wake window 75–120 min, 4 naps per day, total sleep ~14–16 hrs
4 months: wake window 90–120 min, 3–4 naps per day, total sleep ~14–15 hrs
5 months: wake window 90–180 min, 3 naps per day, total sleep ~13–15 hrs
Wake windows are counted from when baby WAKES, not from when they last fell asleep
These are averages — your baby may run 15–30 minutes shorter or longer than these windows
Watch the baby, not the clock — tired cues are more reliable than the timer
warnings: Total sleep includes both naps and overnight — don't be alarmed if night sleep seems short if naps are long
Wide awake windows at 3am are not the same as daytime wake windows — this usually improves by 3–4 months as circadian rhythm matures

sections.title: Sample Daily Schedules
sections.summary: Concrete examples of what an Eat-Play-Sleep day looks like at 1 and 2 months.
tips: 1-month example — 7am wake: 7am feed + play / 8:15am nap / 9:30am feed + play / 11am nap / 12:30pm feed + play / 2pm nap / 3:30pm feed + play / 5pm nap / 6:30pm feed + play / 8pm nap / 9:30pm feed + bedtime
2-month example — 7am wake: 7am feed + play / 8:30am nap / 10am feed + play / 11:30am nap / 1pm feed + play / 2:45pm nap / 4:15pm feed + play / 5:45pm short nap / 7pm feed / 8pm bedtime
These are examples only — a baby's actual schedule drifts by 30–60 minutes either way and that's completely normal
Bedtime at this age is often late (9–10pm) — it shifts earlier naturally around 3–4 months
Don't wake a sleeping baby to match a schedule in the first 4 weeks — let hunger be the guide
A 'schedule' at this age is really just a pattern you notice, not one you enforce

sections.title: Overstimulation Before Sleep
sections.summary: A baby who is too stimulated before sleep will fight it — the wind-down matters as much as the wake window.
tips: Signs of overstimulation: turning away from faces, arching back, increased fussiness, hiccuping, yawning rapidly, inability to settle
Begin winding down 10–15 minutes before the end of the wake window — don't wait for tired cues to start the routine
In the last 10 minutes before sleep: dim lights, lower voices, stop active play, reduce eye contact and stimulation
Avoid screens, loud music, or busy environments right before sleep
Visitors and social situations are some of the biggest sources of overstimulation — watch baby carefully and be willing to take them away for a quiet feed and wind-down
If baby is already overtired and overstimulated: skip the usual routine, go straight to the quietest, darkest, most familiar settling approach you have
warnings: Overstimulated babies can look wired and alert — this is a cortisol response, not a sign they aren't tired
The window to settle an overstimulated baby is much longer — prevention is far easier than rescue
